## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT865]**
> *Differentiate between Optical See-Through HMD and Video See-Through HMD in the context of AR visualization for robotics. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Optical See-Through HMD | Video See-Through HMD |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Path to Eye** | Direct view of the world through a semi-transparent combiner; the virtual content is optically added. | World is captured by cameras, composited digitally, then displayed on opaque screens to the eye. |
| **Latency** | Lower (no camera/screen re-capture) | Higher (camera + display pipeline) |
| **Occlusion Handling** | Hard (no digital depth buffer of reality) | Easy (real depth map is available) |
| **Failure Mode** | Misalignment of the combiner | Camera failure, display lag, sensor–display sync errors |
| **Robot Use Case** | Drone teleoperation, mobile field robot | Surgical robotics, precision assembly |

> **[Examiner's Note]:** Students often write only one line per category. KTU expects *at least three distinct points* for full 3 marks. Mentioning occlusion handling is the highest-weight differentiator.

> ---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERAT865]**
> *Define "Motion-to-Photon Latency" in AR. Why is it critical for robot teleoperation? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Motion-to-Photon Latency $L$ is the time interval between a **change in the real-world state** (e.g., the robot moves) and the **corresponding updated pixel** appearing on the operator's display.

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}
$$

It is critical because:
1. **Cybersickness** appears when $L > 20$ ms.
2. **Closed-loop instability** in teleoperation arises when the operator's commands are based on stale visual feedback — the human acts on a robot position that no longer matches the display.
3. **Reduced situational awareness** leads to collision and safety violations in shared human–robot workspaces.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q3(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT865 — 14 marks, internal choice with Q3(b)]**
> *Explain in detail the various visualization techniques used in Augmented Reality for robotic applications. With neat diagrams, compare the optical see-through and video see-through HMD architectures. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Visualization Techniques Taxonomy (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** AR visualization in robotics is the perceptual fusion of sensor-derived virtual content with the operator's view of the real robot and its environment. The goal is *augmented situational awareness*.

2. **Monitor-Based AR (1 mark):** The simplest paradigm. A 2D screen (laptop, control-station monitor) shows a live camera feed of the robot with overlaid trajectories, sensor heat-maps, and digital-twin annotations. Used in *lab simulation* and *off-line path planning*. Limitation: no depth cues beyond shading.

3. **Handheld / Mobile AR (1 mark):** Smartphones and tablets running ARKit/ARCore. In robotics, used for *field inspection* (e.g., a maintenance engineer views a robot's internal state through a tablet). Limitation: requires the user to hold the device — degrades *Dexterous Teleoperation*.

4. **Optical See-Through HMD (1 mark):** Combiner-based glasses (HoloLens). Used for *drone teleoperation* — the pilot sees the drone in the sky and overlays flight path, battery status, and obstacle warnings. Advantage: direct world view → minimal latency. Disadvantage: hard to achieve *correct occlusion* because no real depth buffer is available.

5. **Video See-Through HMD (1 mark):** VR headsets fitted with front-facing cameras (e.g., Varjo XR-3). The captured real scene is digitally composited with the virtual content. Used in *surgical robotics* and *precision assembly* where proper occlusion of virtual tools by real anatomy is mandatory. Disadvantage: the entire frame must be re-rendered → higher latency.

6. **Projection / Spatial AR (1 mark):** A projector casts virtual annotations directly onto the robot's body or the work-cell floor. Used in *cobot programming* — the projected arrows guide the operator in defining pick-and-place waypoints. No head-mounted display needed.

7. **Tangible / Magic-Lens AR (1 mark):** A tracked handheld lens is moved over a robot's schematic; through the lens, the operator sees internal mechanisms, joint torques, and failure predictions. Used in *robot training and education*.

#### Part (b) — Optical See-Through vs Video See-Through HMD (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

1. **Optical See-Through Architecture (2 marks):**
   - Light from the real world passes *directly* through a partially reflective **combiner prism** or **waveguide** to the user's retina.
   - A micro-projector (LCoS or micro-LED) injects virtual pixels into the same optical path.
   - The two streams combine *additively in the eye*, not digitally.
   - **Equation (depth perceived by user):**
   $$
   D_{\text{ver}} = \frac{1}{Z_{\text{virtual}}}, \quad D_{\text{acc}} = \frac{1}{Z_{\text{real}}}
   $$
   - The user's eye must accommodate at $Z_{\text{real}}$ but converge at $Z_{\text{virtual}}$ — this is the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**.

2. **Video See-Through Architecture (2 marks):**
   - One or two outward-facing cameras capture the real scene.
   - A graphics pipeline (e.g., Unity, Unreal) renders virtual content at 60–120 Hz.
   - A **compositor** applies:
   $$
   C_{\text{final}} = \alpha\,C_{\text{virt}} + (1 - \alpha)\,C_{\text{real}}
   $$
   - The final image is shown on opaque internal displays.
   - Because the display is digital, the device can simulate a correct focal plane using **varifocal** or **multifocal** optics to mitigate VAC.

3. **Comparative Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Optical See-Through | Video See-Through |
|---|---|---|
| Real-world view | Direct, no re-capture | Camera-mediated, digitized |
| Latency | Low (only display side) | High (capture + render + display) |
| Occlusion | Hard | Easy (depth-aware compositing) |
| VAC mitigation | Difficult | Possible (varifocal) |
| Failure mode | Combiner misalignment | Camera/dropout, sensor–display sync error |
| Robot use case | Drone, mobile robot | Surgery, assembly |

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Both paradigms are widely used; the choice depends on whether *occlusion fidelity* (favoring video) or *latency* (favoring optical) is the dominant requirement.

> **[Examiner's Valuation Warning]:** A common mistake is to give a one-line answer like *"Optical is faster than video"*. KTU expects the *underlying reason* (no camera re-capture → less pipeline depth) and the *quantitative consequence* (latency budget).

> ---

> **Q3(b) [Alternative 14-mark question — KTU internal choice pattern]**
> *With a suitable block diagram, explain the complete AR visualization pipeline used in a robot teleoperation system. Derive the pinhole camera projection equation and explain how it is used to register virtual content on a real-world object. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — AR Visualization Pipeline (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw (or describe) a five-stage pipeline:
   - **Scene Capture** → RGB-D camera on the robot.
   - **Tracking** → SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM3) → 6-DoF pose.
   - **Virtual Content Generation** → Digital twin of the robot, waypoints, sensor heat-maps.
   - **Compositing** → Alpha blending using:
   $$
   C_{\text{final}}(u,v) = \alpha(u,v)\,C_{\text{virt}}(u,v) + \bigl(1 - \alpha(u,v)\bigr)\,C_{\text{real}}(u,v)
   $$
   - **Display** → Head-mounted or handheld display.

2. **Stage-by-stage explanation (3 marks):**
   - *Capture*: 30–60 Hz frame rate, with depth at 1280×720 resolution.
   - *Tracking*: feature extraction (ORB), descriptor matching, RANSAC outlier rejection, pose graph optimization.
   - *Virtual Content*: ROS-based digital twin subscriber; receives robot joint states.
   - *Compositing*: software renderer (Unity/ROS-Industrial) uses the **camera intrinsic matrix** $K$ and **extrinsic matrix** $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ for projection.
   - *Display*: 90 Hz OLED panel with 110° FOV.

3. **End-to-end latency budget (1 mark):**
   $$
   L_{\text{total}} = 5 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 19 \text{ ms} \le 20 \text{ ms (OK)}
   $$

#### Part (b) — Pinhole Projection Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **State the projection model (1 mark):**
   A 3D world point $\mathbf{P}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ maps to a 2D image pixel $(u,v)$ through the *intrinsic–extrinsic pipeline*.

2. **World → Camera transform (1.5 marks):**
   $$
   \mathbf{P}_c = R\,\mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t}
   $$

3. **Perspective division (1.5 marks):**
   $$
   x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
   $$

4. **Intrinsic mapping to pixels (1.5 marks):**
   $$
   u = f_x\,x_n + c_x, \qquad v = f_y\,y_n + c_y
   $$

5. **Combined matrix form (1 mark):**
   $$
   s\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
   $$

6. **Application to AR (1 mark):** In a robot teleoperation system, the virtual gripper is defined in *world coordinates* (e.g., $X_w, Y_w, Z_w$). The same projection is applied so that the rendered virtual gripper lands on the exact same pixel $(u, v)$ as the real gripper would, ensuring pixel-accurate registration.

> **[Examiner's Valuation Warning]:** Students frequently *forget the scale factor* $s = Z_c$ or *omit the principal point* $(c_x, c_y)$. Both are mandatory in a 7-mark derivation. Also: state clearly that $R$ is orthonormal ($R^T R = I$); this earns 0.5 extra credit for rigor.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Mark-Loss Reasons in Visualization Questions**
> 1. **Confusing AR with VR** — VR replaces reality, AR augments it. This single error costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $Z_c$ in the denominator** of the pinhole projection — partial marks deducted for "incomplete projection".
> 3. **Mixing Optical and Video See-Through** architectures in the same answer — examiners deduct 0.5 per misplaced concept.
> 4. **Stating latency as a number without derivation** — you must show $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$.
> 5. **Skipping the alpha mask** when describing compositing — the blending equation $C_{\text{final}} = \alpha C_{\text{virt}} + (1-\alpha) C_{\text{real}}$ is *not optional*.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Visualization Techniques for AR (KTU Module 1)**

- **Definition:** AR visualization = real-time fusion of virtual + real, geometrically registered, perceptually consistent.
- **Five Stages:** Capture → Track → Render → Composite → Display.
- **Five Paradigms:** Monitor, Handheld, Optical See-Through HMD, Video See-Through HMD, Projection (Spatial) AR, Tangible/Magic-Lens AR.
- **Optical See-Through:** direct view + combiner prism; *low latency*, *hard occlusion*.
- **Video See-Through:** camera capture + digital compositing; *easy occlusion*, *higher latency*.
- **Compositing Equation (must memorize):**
  $$
  C_{\text{final}} = \alpha\,C_{\text{virt}} + (1-\alpha)\,C_{\text{real}}
  $$
- **Pinhole Projection (must memorize):**
  $$
  s\,[u\ v\ 1]^T = K\,[R\mid t]\,[X_w\ Y_w\ Z_w\ 1]^T
  $$
- **Latency Budget:** $L_{\text{total}} \le 20$ ms.
- **Stereo Depth:** $Z = fB/d$.
- **VAC Threshold:** $\Delta D \le 0.5$ diopter.
- **Field of View:** $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan(d/2f)$.
- **Distortion:** $x_d = x_u(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$.
- **Robotics Use Cases:** Surgical (Da Vinci), Drone teleop (HoloLens), Cobot programming (Projection), Field inspection (Mobile AR), Digital-twin training (Monitor).
- **Reality–Virtuality Continuum (Milgram & Kishino):** AR is between fully real and fully virtual environments.
- **KTU Mnemonic — "C-T-R-C-D"**: **C**apture, **T**rack, **R**ender, **C**omposite, **D**isplay.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Augmented Reality (AR) for Human-Robot Communication** is a spatial computing paradigm that overlays computer-generated, context-aware visual, auditory, and haptic cues onto a human operator's direct view of a robot's physical workspace, thereby mediating and enhancing bidirectional communication between the human and the robot (or a fleet of robots). Within the KTU 2024 PERAT865 framework, this discipline sits at the intersection of **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)**, **Computer Vision**, and **Spatial Registration**.

A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)**, as defined by **ISO/TS 15066:2016**, is a robot designed for direct operation within a shared, fenceless workspace with a human operator, where the robot's motion, force, and intent are explicitly constrained to ensure human safety. When AR is fused with cobotics, the system forms a **Mixed Reality HRI (MR-HRI)** loop, where the digital twin, motion intent, safety zones, and task progress are visually co-located with the physical cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize This):**
> "AR for Human-Robot Communication is the use of registered, real-time, 3D virtual annotations (paths, force vectors, safety bubbles, intent icons) superimposed on a live view of a robot to non-ambiguously convey operational state, task intent, and safety boundaries to a human collaborator — eliminating the need for monitors, joysticks, or 2D HMIs."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chess grandmaster teaching a beginner**. Instead of saying *"move your bishop to that square, then protect the rook, then watch the diagonal"*, you simply hold up a **transparent glass board with glowing arrows drawn on it** directly over the real board. The beginner sees the real pieces, but your arrows, predicted threats, and suggested moves are drawn *in the air, exactly where they belong*.

That is exactly what AR does for humans and cobots:

- The **physical cobot arm** = the chess pieces (the real, working machine).
- The **AR headset / tablet** = the transparent glass board.
- The **floating arrows, force vectors, and safety bubbles** = the master's annotations.
- The **human operator** = the beginner learning and collaborating in real time.

In a factory, before the human walks near the cobot, AR can already show them: *"The robot plans to move HERE in 0.8 seconds. The safe zone is THIS glowing circle. Stop if your hand enters the red region."* This makes the robot's **invisible intentions visible**, which is the entire purpose of AR-based HRI.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics You Must Know for KTU Exams:**
> - **Registration Accuracy**: The spatial error (in **millimeters** or **arc-minutes**) between a virtual object and its real-world anchor. Typical AR-HRI systems target **≤ 2 mm – 5 mm** registration for tabletop cobot cells.
> - **Latency (Motion-to-Photon)**: The end-to-end delay between a robot's physical motion and the update of its virtual avatar on the display. **< 90 ms** is the perceptual threshold to avoid VR sickness / misinterpretation; industrial AR targets **< 20 ms**.
> - **Field of View (FoV)**: Angular extent of the display, typically **30° – 110°** for current AR HMDs (HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2).
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)**: Cobots commonly have **6 or 7 DoF**; AR interfaces must visualize all of them.
> - **Collaborative Operating Modes (ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066)**: Safety-rated Monitored Stop, Hand Guiding, Speed & Separation Monitoring, Power & Force Limiting.

### 1.4 Why AR + Cobots? The Industrial Motivation

| Problem with Traditional Cobot HMI | AR-Based Solution |
|---|---|
| Operator must look away from the cell to read a 2D teach pendant | Gaze stays on the workspace; cues are in-situ |
| Robot's planned trajectory is invisible until execution | Trajectory is drawn as a glowing 3D line *before* motion |
| Safety zones are abstract polygons on a screen | Zones appear as colored, depth-correct bubbles on the floor |
| Programming requires robot-specific language | Operator **demonstrates** the task by physically moving the cobot, and AR confirms the recorded path |
| Status of multiple robots is on a SCADA dashboard | Each robot is tagged in space with floating labels |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Pipeline of an AR-Cobot System

The operation of an AR-mediated cobot cell can be broken into six sequential logic blocks. KTU examiners love to ask "Explain the architecture of an AR-based HRI system" — this is the canonical answer.

1. **Perception & Scene Capture**
   - RGB-D cameras, LiDAR, or the cobot's own proprioceptive encoders capture the state of the workspace.
   - SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) is performed to build a 3D map and localize the HMD / tablet within it.

2. **World Registration (Pose Estimation)**
   - The system computes a transformation matrix that aligns the virtual AR coordinate frame with the real-world cobot frame.
   - Marker-based (fiducials like ArUco) or marker-less (natural feature tracking) methods are used.

3. **Robot State Acquisition**
   - The cobot's forward kinematics stream its joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ to the AR application via ROS (Robot Operating System), OPC-UA, or a TCP socket.

4. **Virtual Scene Generation**
   - The AR engine (Unity, Unreal, or OpenGL/Vulkan) renders the end-effector avatar, the planned trajectory, safety volumes, and task icons using the live joint data.

5. **Compositing & Display**
   - The rendered virtual layer is blended with the live camera / optical see-through feed and presented to the operator on the HMD, tablet, or projection surface.

6. **Bi-directional Interaction**
   - The human issues commands (gesture, gaze, voice, or touch) that the AR system translates into robot motion primitives; the robot's new state re-enters step 3, closing the loop.

### 2.2 The Core Mathematical Backbone

**A. Forward Kinematics of the Cobot (Denavit-Hartenberg Convention)**

For a cobot with $n$ joints, the end-effector pose in the base frame is obtained by chaining homogeneous transformation matrices:

$$T_0^n(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i)$$

where each link transform is

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The AR system uses $T_0^n$ in real time to draw the **virtual avatar of the end-effector** at the correct 3D position. This is the single most important equation for any KTU question on AR-robot visualization.

**B. AR Registration (Camera-to-World Transform)**

The virtual object's position must be projected through the camera's intrinsic matrix $K$ and the camera-to-world pose $T_{CW}$:

$$x_{\text{image}} = K \cdot T_{CW} \cdot X_{\text{world}}$$

The registration error is minimized by solving a non-linear least-squares problem over detected feature correspondences:

$$\min_{T_{CW}} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \rho\left( \left\| \pi(T_{CW} \cdot X_j^{\text{world}}) - x_j^{\text{image}} \right\|^2 \right)$$

where $\pi(\cdot)$ is the perspective projection function and $\rho$ is a robust loss (e.g., Huber) to reject outliers.

**C. Safety Zone Representation**

The collaborative operating space is typically modeled as a sphere or ellipsoid around the end-effector:

$$S = \{ X \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid (X - p)^T E^{-1} (X - p) \le 1 \}$$

where $p$ is the end-effector position and $E$ is a shape matrix encoding the protective radius (e.g., **0.5 m** for Power & Force Limiting per ISO/TS 15066).

**D. Speed & Separation Monitoring (SSM)**

The cobot's speed $v$ must satisfy the protective separation distance criterion:

$$S_p(v) = S_h + S_r + S_{\text{robot}} + S_{\text{intrusion}}$$

where
- $S_h$ = human's reach (typically **0.55 m** per ISO/TS 15066),
- $S_r$ = robot's stopping distance, $S_r = \int_0^{t_s} v(t)\,dt$,
- $S_{\text{robot}}$ = robot's spatial envelope,
- $S_{\text{intrusion}}$ = operator's intrusion into the safety zone.

AR visualizes the live $S_p$ boundary as a colored footprint that grows when the human approaches.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — at least one of these equations appears in every KTU ESE paper for this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | End-effector pose via FK | $T_0^n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i)$ | homogeneous 4×4 | Drives the virtual avatar position |
| 2 | DH link transform | $A_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ as above | — | Use θ, d, a, α from joint table |
| 3 | Camera projection | $x = K \cdot T_{CW} \cdot X$ | pixels / meters | $K$ = intrinsic matrix |
| 4 | Registration error | $\min_{T_{CW}} \sum \left\| \pi(T_{CW}X_j) - x_j \right\|^2$ | mm | Solved via PnP or bundle adjustment |
| 5 | Safety ellipsoid | $(X-p)^T E^{-1} (X-p) \le 1$ | m² | Defines the visual safety bubble |
| 6 | Protective distance SSM | $S_p = S_h + S_r + S_{\text{robot}} + S_{\text{intrusion}}$ | m | ISO/TS 15066 |
| 7 | Robot stopping distance | $S_r = \int_0^{t_s} v(t)\,dt$ | m | $t_s$ = stopping time |
| 8 | Update rate for flicker-free AR | $f \ge 60$ Hz | Hz | Perceptual floor is ~75 Hz |
| 9 | Motion-to-photon latency | $L \le 90$ ms | ms | Below this, AR feels "live" |
| 10 | Field of View (HoloLens 2) | $\text{FoV} \approx 52^\circ$ diagonal | degrees | Smaller than human eye (~200°) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

This pipeline is not academic — it powers real industrial deployments:

- **BMW Spartanburg** uses AR to project the next assembly step onto the engine bay.
- **Universal Robots + AR** allows "ghost teaching": the operator drags the cobot arm physically, and AR draws the recorded path as a glowing tube.
- **NASA's Robonaut 2** uses AR overlays for teleoperation from the ISS.
- **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci)** uses AR to visualize pre-operative imaging fused onto the patient.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 6-DoF Cobot (UR5-Style Arm)

The **UR5** (Universal Robots) is a 6-DoF cobot and a favourite KTU exam example. Let us derive its end-effector pose symbolically. We will simplify by showing the chain in two halves: a 3-DoF positioning wrist and a 3-DoF orienting wrist.

**Step 1 — Read the DH Parameters of the UR5.**

The UR5 uses the standard DH convention with the following table (joints 1 to 6):

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ (m) | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | 0.089159 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | -0.42500 | 0 |
| 3 | $\theta_3$ | 0 | -0.39225 | 0 |
| 4 | $\theta_4$ | 0.10915 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 5 | $\theta_5$ | 0.09465 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ |
| 6 | $\theta_6$ | 0.08230 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Write $A_1$.**

Substituting $i=1$ values into the DH matrix:

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & 0 & s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & 0 & -c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & d_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & 0 & s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & 0 & -c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.089159 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c_i = \cos\theta_i$, $s_i = \sin\theta_i$.

**Step 3 — Write $A_2$.**

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & a_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & a_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & -0.425\,c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & -0.425\,s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial product $A_1 A_2$.**

This multiplication gives the pose of frame 2 relative to the base. Multiplying:

$$A_1 A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 & -c_1 s_2 & s_1 & -0.425\,c_1 c_2 \\ s_1 c_2 & -s_1 s_2 & -c_1 & -0.425\,s_1 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & 0.089159 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Why this step matters:** The third row, third column is **0** — this tells us that, after joints 1 and 2, the $z$-axis of frame 2 is perpendicular to the base $z$-axis, which matches the UR5's offset shoulder geometry. The AR system uses this matrix to draw the **upper-arm avatar** in the correct 3D location.

**Step 5 — Form the full chain $T_0^6 = A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4 A_5 A_6$.**

While the symbolic full product is large, the **logical structure** is:

$$T_0^6 = \underbrace{A_1 A_2 A_3}_{\text{positioning (wrist centre at }p\text{)}} \cdot \underbrace{A_4 A_5 A_6}_{\text{orientation}}$$

The **AR visualization pipeline** uses this in two decoupled parts:

- The **position** $p = [p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$ is read from the 4th column of $A_1 A_2 A_3$ to draw the **end-effector point**.
- The **orientation** $R = A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4 A_5 A_6\,[\text{rotational part}]$ is used to draw a **gripper icon** with the correct roll-pitch-yaw.

**Step 6 — Numerical example.**

Set $\theta_1 = 0$, $\theta_2 = -\pi/2$, $\theta_3 = 0$, $\theta_4 = -\pi/2$, $\theta_5 = 0$, $\theta_6 = 0$ (the "home" pose). Then:

$$p_x = -0.425 \cos(0)\cos(-\pi/2) + \dots = 0$$

$$p_y = 0$$

$$p_z = 0.089159 - 0.425 \sin(-\pi/2) - 0.39225 \sin(0) = 0.089159 + 0.425 = 0.51416 \text{ m}$$

So at the UR5 home pose, the **AR avatar of the end-effector** must be rendered at coordinates $(0, 0, 0.514)$ metres above the base. This is exactly the kind of numerical verification KTU expects in a 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AR Visualization Modalities

| Modality | Display Hardware | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical See-Through HMD** | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2 | Hands-free, 3D-correct depth, mobile | Limited FoV (≈52°), expensive | Long-duration shop-floor tasks |
| **Video See-Through (Tablet)** | iPad / Android tablet with RGB-D | Cheap, large effective FoV (entire screen), easy to develop | Operator must hold device, 2D depth cue only | Inspection, training, small cells |
| **Projection-Based AR** | Ceiling-mounted projector | Hands-free, no headset fatigue, large workspace | Only works on flat surfaces, shadows block occlusion | Painting, drilling, layout marking |
| **Spatial AR / CAVE** | Multiple projectors / displays | Immersive, multi-user | Not portable, expensive | R&D, design review |
| **Handheld Pinch Glove** | Data glove + AR HMD | Natural gesture input | Calibration drift | Kinaesthetic teaching of cobots |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AR Interaction Modalities for Cobots

| Modality | Input Sensor | Robot Command Issued | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand Gesture** | Leap Motion / HoloLens hand tracking | Move, stop, open gripper | Pinch to grip, wave to start |
| **Gaze (Eye Tracking)** | Built-in HMD eye tracker | Approach gaze point, confirm | "Look at the part, then say 'pick'" |
| **Voice** | Microphone + ASR (e.g., Azure) | High-level tasks | "Pick the red bolt" |
| **Tangible AR** | Tracked fiducial / cube on robot | Switch mode | Flip a virtual switch by tapping |
| **Touch on Tablet** | Capacitive screen | Set waypoints, drag paths | Drag a glowing line to redefine trajectory |

### 3.4 Derivation: Why Motion-to-Photon Latency Must Be Below 90 ms

Human reaction to a visual stimulus begins at the retina, travels via the optic nerve to the visual cortex, and is perceived in roughly **80–100 ms**. If the AR overlay updates later than the robot's actual motion by more than this window, the operator will see a **causal mismatch** — the avatar appears to "lag behind" the real arm. Psychophysics research (Ellis et al., 1990s) established that the threshold of "simultaneity" is approximately:

$$L_{\text{threshold}} \approx 90 \text{ ms}$$

Below this, the visual system fuses the real and virtual motions into a single percept (the **ventriloquist effect**). Above it, the operator notices the lag, trust in the AR drops, and stop-button reaction time increases — a safety hazard in a cobot cell. This is why industrial AR-HRI stacks (e.g., ROS 2 with DDS, Unity with hardware-accelerated rendering) target **end-to-end latency of 10–20 ms**.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a "Cobot" as per ISO/TS 15066. List its four collaborative operating modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)**, per **ISO/TS 15066:2016**, is a robot designed for direct interaction with a human within a defined collaborative workspace, where safety is achieved by the robot's inherent design and control — not by fences or cages.
The four collaborative operating modes are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — robot stops when human enters the workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding (HG)** — operator physically leads the robot.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — robot slows as the human approaches.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — robot's force/torque is capped below biomechanical injury thresholds.
*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for modes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is "registration error" in an AR-HRI system, and what is the typical acceptable value for a tabletop cobot cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Registration error** is the spatial discrepancy (in mm or arc-minutes) between the position/orientation of a virtual AR element and the real-world feature it is supposed to be aligned with. It arises from inaccuracies in camera intrinsics, SLAM drift, latency, and calibration.
For a tabletop cobot cell, the typical acceptable registration error is **≤ 2 mm to 5 mm** in translation and **≤ 1°** in orientation. Exceeding this makes it unsafe to align the virtual safety zone with the real robot's envelope. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sources of error, 1 mark for values.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)
